ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general biryani class (FOODON_00002572). No specific baigan/brinjal biryani subclass exists; closest_parent match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP covers biryani as a rice-based mixed dish. No eggplant-specific biryani code exists in FoodEx2; closest_parent match applied.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept found for baigan biryani or biryani as a food product. SNOMED CT is not used for this record.

main_ingredients: spices (cumin, coriander, turmeric, garam masala, chilli)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving of baigan biryani is approximately 350 g, comprising a generous portion of spiced basmati rice layered with tandoor-cooked brinjal, typically served with raita and gravy on the side.

nutrient_source.title: Vegetable Biryani / Mixed Vegetable Rice Dish — ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017), adapted with USDA FoodData Central eggplant and basmati rice reference values
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN); USDA Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for baigan biryani exists in ICMR-NIN. Values are adapted from ICMR-NIN vegetable biryani / mixed vegetable rice composite (approximately 60% cooked basmati rice, 25% brinjal, 10% yoghurt/raita component, 5% oil/ghee and spices). Eggplant nutrient profile cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central FDC ID 169228 (eggplant, raw) and USDA FDC ID 169704 (rice, white, long-grain, cooked). Macros are consistent with published Indian vegetable biryani composites. Values represent a plausible per-100 g estimate for the as-served dish.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition; basmati rice GI reference (GI ~58) used as primary analogous anchor for rice-dominant mixed dish
gi_source.organization: American Journal of Clinical Nutrition / University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for baigan biryani. The GI of 57 is estimated using basmati rice as the primary glycemic driver (GI ~58 per University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021 international tables). The presence of brinjal (low GI vegetable), yoghurt (GI ~36), and fat/protein from ghee and spices moderates the overall GI slightly below plain basmati rice. Glycemic load calculated as: GI (57) × available carbohydrate per serving (82.25 g from 350 g × 23.5 g/100 g) ÷ 100 = 46.9. This is a high GL per serving, consistent with a large rice-dominant restaurant portion.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content approximately 310 mg per 100 g; a full 350 g restaurant serving delivers approximately 1085 mg sodium
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to half a standard restaurant serving and request reduced salt and ghee preparation, as the sodium contribution from spice blends and added salt can approach 50% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg in a single meal.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic load per serving (GL ~47) driven by a large basmati rice portion; carbohydrate content approximately 82 g per standard restaurant serving
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with type 2 diabetes should consume a reduced portion (150–200 g) of baigan biryani, pair it with additional low-GI vegetables or raita, and monitor postprandial blood glucose, as the high carbohydrate load from basmati rice can cause significant glycaemic excursions even though basmati rice has a medium GI.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from ghee (approximately 1.4 g per 100 g) and moderate sodium; brinjal provides beneficial dietary fibre and antioxidants
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For individuals with cardiovascular disease, baigan biryani can be consumed occasionally in a controlled portion (150–200 g) prepared with minimal ghee and no added salt, as the saturated fat and sodium content of a full restaurant serving may adversely affect lipid profiles and blood pressure; the brinjal component provides beneficial soluble fibre and nasunin antioxidants.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Baigan Biryani (also known as Baingan Biryani or Brinjal Biryani) is a fragrant Indian rice dish in which brinjal (eggplant / aubergine) is marinated in a blend of yoghurt and aromatic spices — including cumin, coriander, turmeric, garam masala, and chilli — then cooked in a tandoor or oven before being layered with long-grain basmati rice and slow-cooked using the traditional dum method. The dish is entirely vegetarian and is a popular North Indian preparation served at restaurants across India and in the Indian diaspora communities of Singapore. The basmati rice contributes a delicate floral aroma and a slightly firm, separate-grain texture, while the tandoor-cooked brinjal develops a smoky, tender character that absorbs the spice marinade deeply. The dish is typically served with raita (a cooling yoghurt-based condiment) and a spiced gravy. Nutritionally, baigan biryani provides approximately 148 kcal per 100 g, with carbohydrates as the dominant macronutrient (~23.5 g/100 g) from the rice, moderate protein (~3.8 g/100 g) from yoghurt and rice, and moderate fat (~4.2 g/100 g) from ghee and cooking oil. The brinjal component contributes dietary fibre (~1.8 g/100 g), potassium, and antioxidants including nasunin. The glycemic index is estimated at 57 (medium), but the glycemic load per standard restaurant serving (~350 g) is high (~47), making portion control important for individuals managing type 2 diabetes or insulin resistance. Sodium content is moderate to high (~310 mg/100 g) due to spice blends and added salt, which is relevant for individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The dish is gluten-free and halal-suitable, making it accessible to a broad range of dietary preferences in Singapore's multicultural food landscape.
common_user_queries: How many calories in eggplant biryani?
Is baingan biryani good for diabetics?
What is the GI of biryani?
What spices are in brinjal biryani?
How is baigan biryani different from chicken biryani?
Can I eat biryani if I have high blood pressure?
What is the sodium content of biryani?

confidence_reason: No exact entry for baigan biryani exists in ICMR-NIN or any other approved primary source. Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN vegetable biryani composites and USDA FoodData Central ingredient references for eggplant and basmati rice. GI is estimated by analogy from basmati rice GI data in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021. Confidence is medium because the adaptation is methodologically defensible but not based on a direct laboratory measurement of this specific dish.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are estimated from ICMR-NIN vegetable biryani composite data and USDA FoodData Central ingredient references (eggplant FDC 169228; white long-grain rice cooked FDC 169704). No direct laboratory analysis of baigan biryani as served at Singapore restaurants is available in any approved source. 2. trans_fat_g is reported as 0.0; this is confirmed zero per USDA reference data for the component ingredients — ghee contains negligible industrially produced trans fats, and naturally occurring ruminant trans fats in ghee are at trace levels below reporting thresholds in the ICMR-NIN and USDA sources. 3. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0; this is naturally zero as baigan biryani is a vegetarian dish with no meat, fish, or egg components — the only animal-derived ingredient is yoghurt/ghee which contributes negligible B12 at the quantities used. 4. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN vegetable biryani composite or the USDA ingredient references used for this adaptation. 5. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source or the USDA eggplant and rice references used for this adaptation. 6. GI of 57 is an analogous estimate based on basmati rice as the primary glycemic driver; no direct GI measurement of baigan biryani exists in the University of Sydney GI Database or peer-reviewed literature as of the accessed date. 7. Serving size of 350 g is based on typical North Indian restaurant biryani portion sizes observed across the three field survey data restaurants listed in the source record. 8. The food_id GD000089 is from the field survey data seed record and is not present in the FD series food_index.json. 9. Ontology codes: FoodOn FOODON_00002572 and FoodEx2 A0CKP are closest_parent matches for biryani as a class; no eggplant-specific biryani subclass exists in either ontology as of the accessed date.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 1: Drafted full record from ICMR-NIN and USDA sources. Identified need to verify aliases_by_language consistency with flat aliases list.
Iteration 2: Verified aliases_by_language entries against flat aliases. Corrected ontology match types to 'closest_parent'. Confirmed zero-value and sentinel-value justifications in notes.

Iteration 3: Identified root cause of q8 failure — Hindi entry in aliases_by_language contained a Cyrillic character (я) mixed into Devanagari script, producing a string that did not match the pure Devanagari 'बैंगन बिरयानी' in the flat aliases list. Corrected aliases_by_language.hindi[0] to pure Devanagari 'बैंगन बिरयानी'. Performed complete verbatim cross-check of all aliases_by_language entries against flat aliases array. All entries now consistent.
